Description: Unique identifier for the source study .
Unique identifier for a single experiment within a study.
Unique identifier assigned to each participant.
Presentation order within the session (0-indexed). The order in which the participant saw this trial.
The intended word of the picture presented on the trial; participants were instructed to produce this word as their response.
The distractor word presented together with the target picture in the picture-word interference paradigm.
Time taken by the participant to respond, in milliseconds.
Binary indicator of whether the participant's response was correct.
Language of the experimental materials and task instructions: "EN" (English) or "DE" (German).
Participant's native or first-acquired language.
Whether the experiment included a familiarization phase in which participants were exposed to the target pictures before the main task: "yes" or "no".
Stimulus onset asynchrony between distractor and target-picture onset, in milliseconds (negative values indicate that the distractor preceded the picture).
Whether participant responses were produced overtly (spoken aloud) or covertly (silently identified, indicated by button press): "overt" or "covert".
Whether participants performed additional tasks beyond picture naming in the same session: "yes" or "no".
Whether the picture-naming task was embedded in a gamified interface: "yes" or "no".
Data collection modality: "online" or "offline" (in-person lab).
Congruency between target and distractor: "congruent" (distractor is the target word itself) or "incongruent".
Categorical (semantic) relationship between target and distractor: "related", "unrelated", or NA when the contrast was not manipulated in this experiment.
Associative relationship between target and distractor: "related", "unrelated", or NA when the contrast was not manipulated in this experiment.
Phonological relationship between target and distractor: "related", "unrelated", or NA when the contrast was not manipulated in this experiment.
Presentation modality of the distractor word: "text" (visually superimposed or adjacent to the picture) or "audio" (spoken).
